story:
Benjamin was the butt of everyone’s jokes, not because he was dumb, just livin’ on some other planet. The town council didn’t like a 26‑year‑old sittin’ all day on the courthouse bench, so they gave him a job polishin’ the stack of cannonballs by the Civil War 10‑pounder. A week later Benjamin up and quit. “I found my own cannonball and I’m goin’ into business for myself,” he told the mayor. Folks hooted at that.

Then it got quiet. Benjamin had his nose in a book he’d taken from Ralph’s Barber Shop—H. G. Wells, that old time‑travel story. No one minded, long as he wasn’t botherin’ anybody. I’d always liked him, so I told him to try the library, help carry boxes, read a bit. “Wells was an interestin’ writer,” I said. “These days there’s lots of science fiction, but he near about invented time and space travel.”

“Did he invent it ’cause he was unhappy? ’Cause people laughed at him?” Benjamin asked.

“Naw,” I said. “Probably ’cause it was more excitin’ than what was goin’ on.”

“Whyn’t more people do that? Solve their problems by goin’ somewheres else?”

“Good idea, Benjamin,” I laughed. “Look what the President says—we’re gonna get to the moon yet.””

I didn’t know that would set him off. Benjamin stopped hangin’ around town. When I finally saw him, he was all keyed up. “Can’t talk,” he said, and ran off. I realized he was headed straight for his dream, fixin’ to haul the old Bradford outhouse atop whatever contraption he’d picked for his time machine.

A week later, drivin’ my mail route where the state road bends by Amos Bradford’s, I came across a heap of corrugated metal, two‑by‑fours, tractor parts—junk piled high. On top sat the old Bradford ouhouse. Its door swung shut and I swear I heard a latch click.

“Hey!” I yelled. “That you, Benjamin?”

“Can’t talk now. Busy.”

“You’re not goin’ to hurt yourself, are you?”

“I discovered the newest form of gettin’ around. Time travel. I’m on my way, but I may come back.”

“Why would you leave Bellows Falls?” I asked.

“’Cause everyone thinks I’m dumb as dirt! I’ll show them I ain’t!” he hollered through the door.

I climbed back in my truck, figured best to leave well enough alone. Days passed. No one had seen him. The sheriff asked around, but Benjamin didn’t have family—no mother or father—so the questions dried up. Folks said he’d lit out for somewhere else.

Years rolled on. Sometime in the 1990s I was in a Burlington hotel lobby, picked up a newspaper, and there was Benjamin’s picture. He didn’t look a day older than when he’d vanished in ’66 or ’67, just smilin’ big. A woman stood by him, a baby in his arms. The little caption read, “Here today and gone tomorrow. Benjamin Salt visited Burlington with his family last week. He said he was planning on investing in local businesses because the area promises to have a great future.”

That Benjamin always was somethin’. I tucked the paper under my arm, thinkin’ how I’d show the boys back home. Bet they’re sorry they tore apart his contraption after he left. Turns out he’d found his own cannonball, all right—not iron, but a way out. He didn’t run from our town so much as outgrow it, and then he came back just long enough to prove he’d never been dumb at all—just headed where the rest of us couldn’t see yet.
